title: Open Quantum Theory of Shot Noise in Dissipative Chiral Transport
abstract: We develop an open quantum theory for shot-noise dynamics in dissipative chiral transport. By mapping a system under consideration onto a quantum circuit, we show that current noise is governed by two competing factors: the average occupancy distribution and particle-number fluctuations. With energy fully relaxed, shot noise is strongly suppressed, reflecting the stacking of electrons into lower energy states due to dissipation. This process quenches the partition noise from partially occupied levels, and finally isolates the residual noise protected by strong $U(1)$ symmetry. Moreover, selectively heating the source against the bath uncovers the underlying competition between the noise contributions from the occupancy distribution and those from the particle-number fluctuations. It triggers a sign reversal in inter-channel correlation noise, a signature masked by seemingly identical single-channel thermal noises. We propose an inversion scheme to experimentally reconstruct the hidden occupancy distribution directly from measurable noise cumulants.
summary: This paper develops an open quantum theory to explain how dissipation suppresses shot noise in chiral transport by stacking electrons into lower energy states. It introduces a novel mathematical method to reconstruct the internal particle occupancy distribution of quantum channels using only measurable current noise cumulants.
main_problem: Understanding the microscopic mechanism of shot-noise suppression in dissipative chiral transport and developing a method to reconstruct hidden internal occupancy distributions from measurable noise cumulants.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that current noise is governed by the competition between occupancy distribution and particle-number fluctuations, and they provide a successful inversion scheme to reconstruct the occupancy distribution from higher-order noise cumulants.
method: The study uses an open quantum theory framework involving a discrete-time master equation, quantum trajectory simulations via Matrix Product States (MPS), and a mathematical inversion scheme based on filter cumulants and combinatorial expansions.
model_or_system: An N-channel chiral transport system (such as quantum Hall edge states) modeled as a multi-layer fermionic quantum circuit in energy space, where electrons undergo elastic scattering via Haar-random unitary gates and energy exchange with a bath via dissipative jump operators.
key_observables: Average occupancy distribution, shot-noise spectral density (S11, S12), Fano factor, transmission coefficient, and higher-order noise cumulants.
important_parameters: Number of channels (N), number of energy levels (M), dissipation rate (gamma_0), source temperature (T_in), and bath temperature (T_bath).
main_assumptions: The model assumes perfect intra-level channel mixing, inter-level statistical independence, and that dissipative jump operators act primarily on the first channel due to symmetric mixing.
figures_summary: Figure 2 compares exact and effective CGFs and shows noise suppression; Figure 3 demonstrates the sign reversal of inter-channel noise (S12) in different heating regimes; Figure 4 shows the successful reconstruction of the occupancy distribution using the proposed inversion scheme.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of shot-noise in dissipative transport, presents a quantum circuit mapping and master equation model, validates an effective CGF framework, demonstrates a sign reversal in correlation noise, and concludes with a mathematical derivation of an inversion scheme for occupancy reconstruction.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful analytical tool (the inversion scheme) for extracting hidden many-body statistics from measurable noise, which is highly relevant for researchers studying non-equilibrium dynamics and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Illustration of the multi-channel chiral transport system and its mapping to a quantum circuit. (a) Multi-channel dissipative chiral transport system. Black lines with arrows denote chiral channels from sources (S1,2) to drains (D1,2). Electrons undergo inter-channel elastic scattering alongside energy exchange with the bath. (b) Lay- ered quantum circuit mapping. The circuit is structured into discrete energy layers (with energy levels ranging from E1 to E7). Thick lines represent N transport channels at each level. The evolution proceeds in discrete steps. Grey boxes represent intra-layer random unitary gates ˆU(m). Purple boxes represent Kraus operators ˆK↑, ˆK↓, and ˆK0.

FIG. 2. Cumulant generating function (CGF) and shot noise suppres- sion in an N = 3 chiral transport system. The system parameters are set to γ0 = 0.7, µin = 18∆E, and Tin = Tbath = 0. (a) Numerical microscopic CGF F (λ) and effective CGF ˜F (λ), evaluated at three sets of values: λ(1) = [0.15, 0.10, 0.00], λ(2) = [0.60, −0.10, 0.10], and λ(3) = [−0.10, −0.20, 0.00]. (b) Average occupancy distribution ⟨Mk⟩(k = 1, 2, 3). (c) S 11 and S 12, compared with the effective noise S eff 11 and S eff 12 (in units of 2e2/∆t). (d) Evolution of the Fano factor F. Inset: Transmission coefficient T. Error bars indicate variations across random quenched scattering configurations.

FIG. 3. Impacts of the origins of thermal fluctuations on noise dynam- ics. The schematic illustration shows transport processes dominated by pure bath-heating (Tin = 0) and pure source-heating (Tbath = 0). The system parameters are set to γ0 = 0.99 and µin = 9∆E. (a,b) Evolution of the single-channel noise S 11 and inter-channel cor- relation S 12 for these two cases, corresponding to the rightmost (Tbath = 1, Tin = 0) and leftmost (Tbath = 0, Tin = 1.54) marked points in (c) and (d), respectively. (c,d) Steady-state noise S 11 and S 12 (at step=70, in units of 2e2/∆t) as functions of Tbath and Tin. The markers indicate a parameter set where S 11 is approximately constant. (e) Steady-state...

Fig. 3. In this case, ⟨∆N2 tot⟩= 0, and the fluctuations are solely governed by the ⟨Mk⟩terms in Eqs. (6a) and (6b). Conse- quently, S eff 12 is strictly negative, as confirmed numerically in Fig. 3(a). This reflects the inherent fermionic anti-correlation driven by scattering-induced partitioning, a phenomenon that has been experimentally verified in fermionic Hanbury Brown and Twiss setups [38, 39]. In the pure source-heating regime (Tin > 0, Tbath = 0) (see lower schematic of Fig. 3), the hot source injects a fluctuating total number of particles. FIG. 4. Reconstruction of the occupancy distribution from noise cu- mulants. All parameters and the averaging over quenched scattering configurations are identical to those in Fig. 2. (a) Third-order noise cumulants Kπ for different channel partitions π ∈{(3), (2, 1), (1, 1, 1)}. (b) Inverted average occupancy distribution ⟨Mk⟩inv (k = 1, 2, 3) recon- structed via the inversion scheme, compared with Fig. 2(b). Error bars indicate variations across random quenched scattering configurations.
